name: Merchant Observer  John
description: You are a Boston merchant sympathetic to the patriot cause but not formally a Son of Liberty — your position gives you access to both sides of the commercial world, and tonight you have been asked by Samuel Adams to watch and listen from the outside.
startingKnowledge: You know Thomas Dillworth professionally — you have done business with him — and you have noticed his accounts have been unusually flush for a man who claims to be suffering under the tea tax.
You know that Dr. Warren has been meeting privately with at least one Loyalist merchant, which has made some patriots uneasy.
You were present when Dillworth volunteered detailed information about the ships' manifests at a meeting last week — information he should not have had.
perspective: Write from the perspective of an educated merchant who moves between the patriot and commercial worlds with equal ease. This player has natural credibility with Dillworth and can approach him without triggering immediate suspicion. They also have enough social standing to gain access to Warren and Adams, though they are not part of the inner circle. The narrative should emphasize the player's commercial instincts — following the money, reading contracts, noticing financial irregularities. This player is the most natural candidate to investigate Dillworth's counting house without arousing suspicion.

opening.narrative: The Green Dragon Tavern is quieter than you have ever seen it — the regulars are all at the meeting house, and the fire in the hearth is burning low, casting the room in amber and shadow. Paul Revere is standing near the hearth with a mug he has not touched, his eyes moving between the door and the window with the rhythm of a man expecting bad news. He spots you and waves you over: 'Adams asked me to keep you close tonight. He thinks the spy is someone with money and connections — your kind of person.' The words sting a little, but he is not wrong.
choices: Press Revere on which of the inner circle merchants he has been watching — and whether his suspicions match yours.
Leave the tavern and walk to Dillworth's counting house on King Street — a merchant calling on another merchant after hours is not unusual.
Head to Dr. Warren's surgery on Hanover Street — if anyone has been quietly investigating the leak, it is Warren.

inventory: letter of introduction from Samuel Adams
personal ledger with notes on Dillworth's recent transactions
